content.full_text:
June 7, 1990

California Fair Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Attention: Kevin S. Braaten-Moen, Political Reform Consultant

Re: Your letter to CCTA of June 4, 1990
Your File No A-90-307

Dear Mr. Braaten-Moen:

Based on our discussion of June 6th concerning the above captioned letter, I would like to request an additional clarification.

In determining what constitutes a lobbying contact, if an individual is trying to influence administrative action in a workshop which is consistent with Regulation 18239 (d)(1), i.e., the workshop is: conducted as an open, public hearing for which public notice is given; of which a record is created in a manner which makes possible the creation of a transcript; and for which full public access is provided to this record or transcript or to all written material which is submitted to be part of the record; then that is an exempt activity and would not constitute a contact.

On the other hand, if any one of those three conditions of Regulation 18239 (d)(1) are not met, then participation by an individual in such a workshop would constitute a lobbying contact for FPPC purposes and would go against the 25 contacts within a two month period which may require registration.

I would appreciate your confirming whether my present understanding, as stated above, is correct.

Additionally, we appreciate and strongly support your suggestion that your staff will analyze Regulation 18616 concerning limiting disclosure in connection with these CPUC workshops.

Very truly yours,

Alan J. Gardner

AJG:fm
